Fern Polrrrcer PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329

March 29,2007
Robin L. Clauson
City ofNewport Beach
3300 Newport Blvd.
Newport Beach, California 92659-891 5
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-07-033
Dear Mr. Clauson:
This letter-is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Newport Beach
City Councilmember Michael Henn regarding the conflict of interest provisilns of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act').I Because we do not have the necessary facts to make a
determination regarding some aspects of your question, we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance.z
QUESTIONS
1. May Councilmember Henn participate in decisions to adopt an ordinance that
may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery
treatrnent facilities in the city of Newport Beach (the,.City',)?
2. May Councilmember Henn serve on a cornmittee that will review existing
laws, examine potential changes to city regulations and state law, and make
recofilmendations to the city council with regard to regulation of sober living group
homes that operate within the citfl
CONCLUSIONS
1. Councilmember Henn may participate in decisions to adopt an ordinance that
may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or risidential recovery
treatment facilities in the City if it is not reasonably foreseeable (substantially likely)ihat
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear attitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 1 I 4; Regulation nizslcS, copy enclosed.)
+"


File No. I-07-033
Page No. 2
the governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on any of his economic
interests.
2. councilmember Henn may serve on a committee that may make
recommendations to the city council.regarding regulation of sober living so"p homes
that operate within the CitS so long it is not riasJnably foreseeable that the committee,s
recommendations regardln_g existing laws, potential changes to Cityregulations and state
law, would have a material financial effect on any ofhis economic interests.
F'ACTS
This letter is written on behalf o4 and with the authorization from
Councilmember Henn, whose business provides general business ua"irory and consulting
seryices to various clients.
One of Mr. Henn's clients is R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, a soleproprietorship
operated by Mrs. Lilah Stangeland. Mrs. Stangeland is also ttre sole trus?ee of Ur" noger
9d !ild'Stangeland Trust. Mr. Henn is compensated byR.E. Stangeland Enterprises.
In addition, the Trust owns approximately 55 percent of a hotding *"*p*y, *rrich owns
and operates various subsidiary businessis, including two retail pharmacies iocated
within the City. DTing a March 26,2007 telephonelonversation with vfr. Henn, he
stated he is currently the director and chief financial officer of the holding company and
four subsidiary businesses owned by the holding company. He also stated he is in the
progess of resigning from those positions in conJunction with a pendintreorganization of
the businesses. Mr. Henn's benefits are not related to the finan&al p#;;ce 
of the
retail pharmacy businesses.
He receives no salary or other income from the holding company, or any of its
zubsidiaries. However, he is provided medical coverage undJ trre trotding 
"o111p*y,,
medical insurance policy- According to councilmembLHenn, his medical benefits from
ttre holding company wilt also be terminated within two to four weeks. None of these
business entities -the 
sole proprietorship, trust holding company or any subsidiary
business-is or is presently anticipated to be the subjecl of any alcision uefoie ttre city
council.
The Cityhas established a Commiffee ("the Committee') made up oftwo council
members, two planning commissioners, and residents of the City to review existing laws
and examine potential changgs to City regulations and state taw-wittr r"g;a1;r"grlation
of sober living group homes both licensed and unlicensed by the state tliat operate within
the City' In addition, in our March lg,2007 telephone conversation, you stated that you
"did not see any reasonable impacts" on the arrangement between the group home clients
gd the pharmacy businesses as the result of any p"ai"g decision bedre the city council.
The Committee's charge is to make recommendaiions to the city council forboth changes
in state and local regulations. The pharmacies sell prescription drugs to the clients of two
of the sober living programs currently operating in the City by virtu"e "f Uiiiing-


File No. I-07-033
Page No. 3
alrangements through thoseprograms. The city council is considering adopting an
ordinance that will impose general regulations that may affect the locition 
^or operations
of sober living businesses or residential treafinent facilities. The regulations would be
ynrelaJed to the provision of services or the dispensing of medicatio]rs to the clients of
those businesses.
Councilmember Henn wishes to know if he will have a disqualirying financial
interest in a city council decision to adopt an ordinance affecting ttre tocJJn or
operations of sober living businesses or residelrtial recovery keatrnent facilities. In
addition, he wishes to know if there are any constraints on iri. ability to re.u" on the
Committeg which will investigate and explore options but will not irave any specific
decision before it.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u goi"ir**t t
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the
following eight-step analysis.
steps one and rwo: (l) Is the individuar a .lubric official', and (2) is the
individual making, participating in making, or influenting a governmental decision?
The first two steps in the analysis are not at issue in your letter. As a city council
member, Mr. Henn is a "member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agerrcyf'9{,.therefore, is a "public official" subject to the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act. r (Section g204g; Regulation 1g701(a).) Additionally,
councilmember Henn will be making, participating in making or influen iiig u
govemmental decision if he makes recommendations to the city council or b"ecomes
involved in decisions to adopt an ordinance that imposes general regulations afitecting the
location or operations of sober living businesses or residitial recoiery treatnent
facilities. (Regulations I 8702.1 and 19702.2.)
3 Recusal Re.luirelelts: If apublic official is enumerated in section g7200 (including city
council members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he
or she musr (1) irnmediately prior to the discussion-of the item, orally identify "*il qr; of e"onomic
interest involved in the decision as well as details ofthe economic inierest, asdlscussed in regutation
18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himsglf slhercelf; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, corcent calendars, abserces and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation lg7o2.S,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section g7105.)


File No. I-07-033
Page No. 4
Step Three: What are the economic interests that might be a source of a
conflict of interest?
This step involves identifuing the economic interests that may be impacted by a
goveflrmental decision. Under section 87103 of the Act, there are sii different ways of
identifring economic interests in order to determine if a governmental decision may
result in a conflict of interest for a public officiat. The economic interests pertinent to
your question are as follows:
1. Brtir"ts Enjitv Inrr"tt*"rrt. E-plo)r-*t or M*un**t: An official has an
economic interest in a business entity in which he or she h* , dir*t or indirect
invesknent of $2,000^ormore (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a);; or in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trusteg employeE, orholds -yporifro'or
management (Section 87 1 03 (d); Regulation 1 9703. I (b).
Parent-Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity: The Act also recognizes
that: "[a]n official has an economic interest in a business entity which is a parent or
subsidiary o{, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in which the official has one of
the interests defined in Government code section sltoi(a) or (d).,,
Regulation 18703.1(d)(l) defines a parent-subsidiary relationship as follows:
'?arent-subsidiary. A parent-subsidiary relationship exists when one corporation
directly or indirectlyowns shares possessing more than 50 percent of the voting power of
another corporation."
In your letter, you state that Councilmember Henn has a business that provides
general business advisory and consulting services. You also state tfrut frl i, ,r11*tfy u
director and chief financial officer ortne notoing company and its subsidiary businesses.
We assume he has an investrnent of $2,000 or more in his consulting business. If so, he
has an economic interest in his business. In addition, since he is a director and CFo of
the holding company and its subsidiaries, he has an economic interest in the holding
company and its subsidiaries by virtue of his business position. (Section gZiO:tal.l
Furthermore, if the relationship between the holding company and its subsidiaries
meets the definition ofparent-subsidiary set forth above, Councilmember Henn will also
have an economic interest in these entities, by virtue of ihe parent-subsidiary relationship
among the businesses.
Your facts indicate that in the near future CouncilmemberHenn will be resigning
as director and CFo of the holding company and its various subsidimies. Once he
resigns these positions, Mr. Henn will cease to have a potentially disqualiffint ecorromic
interest in these entities due to his business position. fror"u"., he will continue to have
an economic interest in these businesses as source of income as discussed below.


FileNo. l-07-033
Page No. 5
2. Sources of Income: An official also has an economic interest in anyperson
from whom he or she has received income aggegating$500 within 12 months irior to
the time the relevant govemmental decision ismade. (Section 87103(c); Regulation
r8703.3.)
RE. Stangeland Enterprises/holding company: Forpurposes ofthis analysis, we
assume that Councilmember Henn has received $500 or morl in income from
compensation paid to him by R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, and from the holding company
in the form of medical insurance payments.a (Section 82030.) Thereforg both are
sonrces of income to him. Please note that income aggregating$SO0 or more within 12
months prior to the time a decision is made is disquaifuing. (Section g7103(c).)
Holding Company Subsidiaries:
According to your facts, the holding company owns and operates various
subsidiarybusinesses, including two retail pharmacies located within the city. Ifthe
relationship meets the definition ofparent-subsidiary set forth abovg ttre troiaing
company and in each of its subsidiaries-including the pharmacies-will be deemed
sources of income to CouncilmemberHenn.
Mrs. Lilah Stangeland:
Assuming that Councilmember Henn receives $500 or more in income from R.E.
Stangeland Enterprises, it is a source of income to him. In addition, R.E. Stangeland
Enterprises is solely owned by Mrs. Lilah Stangeland. Where a business entiti is solely
owned or closelyheld, we have advised that the controlling business owner, in tfts casi
Mrs. Stangeland, is also a source of income to the official,-and the official maynot
participate in any decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on her. (Talley Advice Letta,No. A-96-204; Hintschke Advicefetter, No. A-g0-
069; Kathe Advice Letter, No. I-91-507.)
Roger and Lilah Stangeland Trust:
Furthermorg the holding company is 55 percent owned by the Roger and Lilah
Stangeland Trust, ofwhich Mrs. Stangeland is the sole trustee. Witf, r"gid to disclosing
interests held in a trust, an individual has a direct, indirect or beneficial interest in a trust
pursuant to the Act ifthe individual is:
"(1) A trustor and:
"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;
o Under Section 82030 "income" is defined as "a pa;moent received, including but not limited to
any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, inchdln! * rro of food
or beverage, Ioan, forgiveness orpa)ment of indebtedness received ty *re nter, reimbursLent for
experuies, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an
employer, and including any communityproperty interest in tni acomJof a spouse.",


File No. I-07-033
Page No. 6
"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or principal
of the trust orretained any reversionary orremainder interest; oi
"(c) Has retained or reserved any power of appointrnent, including
but not limited to the power to change the rusLe, or the power to
amend, alter or designate, either alone or in conjunction with
anyone else, the person or persons who shall possess or enjoy the
trust property or income.
"(2) A beneficiary and:
"(A) Presently receives income; or
"(B) Has an irrevocabre future right to receive income or
principal ...."
If Mrs. Stangeland has an interest in the tnrst pursuant to terms of Regulation
18234(c), then the assets of the Roger and Lilah stangeland Trust, would be considered
as assets ofMrs- Stangeland as an indivi&n1. Qaltey Advice Letter, No. A-96-204;
Silver Advice Leffer, No. A-96-066; Irmas Advice Letter, A-g2-lgg.) As noted above,
since Mrs. stangeland is a source of income to councilmember He;, he maynot
participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on her.
3. Personal Finances: Councilmember Henn also has an economic interest in his
personal finances and those ofhis immediate family. @egulation 1s703.5.) ..A
govemmental decision will have an effect on this 
""o.rorni. 
interest if the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the ofEcial or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasin g.- (Ibid.) A reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on apublic official's personal finances is maierial if it is at least $250 in any lZ-
rnonth period.s @egulation t aZOS.S;
Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmember Henn,s personal
finances as the resultof the upcoming decisions, but note that a conflict of interest may
arise if it reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on his
personal finances.
- 
5 
-U- nder regulation 18705.5, when deterrnining whether a governmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interestt his or her"penonal finances, neither a financial
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a
direct or indirect investrnent interest shall be considered.


File No. I-07-033
Page No. 7
Step Four: Are these economic interests directly involved in the
governmental decision ?
Councilmember Henn's consulting business, R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, Lilah
Stangeland, the holding company, the pharmacies, and other iubsidiary btisin"sses:
A person, including business entities, sources of income and sources of gifts are
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when, directly or by an agent,
it:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal,or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named pffiy in, or is the subject o{, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official,s-agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involveJthe-
issuancg renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Regulation 187 0a J @).)
If a source of income is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, it is
considered indirectly involved. (Regulation I 3704. I (b).)
You stated in your letter that "[n]one of these business interests-the sole
proprietorship, trust ho]{ing company 9, ary subsidiary business-is or is presently
anticipated to be the subject of any decisionbefore the Newport Beach City Council.,, In
addition, you have not provided any facts that indicate that Councilmember Henn,s
consulting business, R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, orMrs. Stangeland hersel{, would be
the subject of any decision before the city council.
Thereforq based on the facts presented, these sources of income are deemed
indirectly involved in the decisions.
Personal Finances:
If there is any financial effect on the personal finances of a public official or his or
her immediate family they are deemed to be directly involved in a decision. 
-@egulation
18704-5.) Because you have not presented any facis indicating a personal financial
effect, we do not analyze this factor.
steps tr'ive and six: \ilil the financial effect of the decision on
Councilmember Henn's economic interest be material, and reasonably foreseeable?
Once apublic official identifies his orherrelevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether itis reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one of those economic interests by applying the applicable materiality


FileNo. I-07-033
Page No. 8
standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulations 1S700(bX5) and I g705, et
seq.) After finding the applicable materiality standard, the officiaf *uri then decide
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation
18700(bx6).)
Forpersons who are sources of incomg the applicable materiality standard varies
depending on the financial size of the business entityin question.
Indirectllt Invo lved Business Entities :
For business entities that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision,
Regulation I 8705. I (c) provides the materiality standards.
Subdivision (c)(4) ofthis regulation applies to small business entities, and if any
of the business entity economic interests identffied above meet the following criteria in
this subdivision, the financial effects ofthe decisions will be.tnaterial,,: 
a
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
desrease in the.business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,
"(c) The governmental decision wil resurt in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Regulation I g705. I (cX4).)
,u
Under Regulation 18705.3, an effect of a decision is material as to an individual
who is a source of income to an official if any of the following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual,s income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual,s real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Titl;2,calirornia
Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b).,,
The above materiality standard is applicable to Mrs. Lilatr Stangeland, who is a
source of income to Councilmember Henn.
6 For sources of income that are business entities, the materiality standards are the same as those,
set forth above, for a general economic interest in a business entity.


File No. I-O7-033
Page No. 9
Foreseeabilitv:
Generally, the foreseeability..analysis must be applied on a decision-by-decision
basis' Regulation 18706 provides: "A material financial effect on an economic interest is
reasonably foreseeablg within the meaning of Government Code section g7lo3,if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards (see Regulation s 1g704
and 18705) applicable to that economic interest wiil 6emet as a result ofthe
governmental decision.,'
Without specific details regarding an upcoming decision, we cannot provide
guidance as to when it is reasonably foreseeabie that tf,e above *ut".iufity rt*a*d, *itl
be met with regard to Councilmember Henn's various economic interests, including the
holding company and its subsidiaries, orthepharmacies, which sells prescription drugs to
clients of two sober living programs in the ci-ty.
For instancg if the decisions involving the ordinance impact the location or
operations of sober living facilities to the extent that it results in the termination of the
arrangement between the clients of the group homes and the pharmacies, oiif tfr"
decision will have a financial effect on tt e pharmacies of $zti,O0o "r *or", o, affect the
assets of Mrs' Stangeland by $1,000 or morg then it is reasonably foreseeable that the
above materiality standards will be met.
However, the Commission cannot make the determination of forseeability.
UltimatelS the determination of whether ornot it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard will be met is a factual question for the councilmember io decide.
Personal Finances,: An impact on an o{ficial's personal finances is considered to
be'?naterial" if it amounts to $25ooryore in any trrelie-monthperiod. @egulation
18705.5.) You have not provided any information regarding pot;tial i;;; .,
councilmember Henn's personal finances, therefore ri" *" unable to reach a
determination as to this issue.
steps seven and Eight: The *pubric GeneralryD and.,Legany Required
Participation,, Exceptions
An official who otherwise has a conflict ofinterest in a decision may still
participate under the-'fublic ge,nerally'' exception. This exception applies ."t * tt "
financial effect of a decision on a public official's *ro*i" interests is substantially the
same as the effect o1_lsignrficant segment of the public. (see section sr03;
Regulations I 8707-1 8707.10.)
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official,s participation
in a governmental decision is legallyrequired. lSection 87101; Regulation lg70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
Councilmember Henn's situation; therefore, we dJnot address them here. Without


File No. I-07-033
Page No. l0
further information regarding the specific decisions that may arise, we are unable to
render specific advice.
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322_s660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
